title: Getting Started
note: CapCut course/foundation lesson — educational, not a single recreatable effect. Kept as a technique reference.
narration_transcript: A green screen is a very common piece of the VFX puzzle but it can also be the most intimidating, whether you're a filmmaker, content creator or just curious about how visual effects are made you're in the right place. Green screen technology is a powerful tool in the creative industry. Allowing you to transform your storytelling and bring your wildest imaginations to life and they will be diving into a lot of details because understanding the nuances really does make a difference. We want you to know that it's totally okay if you're just starting out. You might be thinking this sounds complicated but don't worry our goal is to make it as user friendly as possible and make sure to include simple examples to get you up and running quickly from choosing your green screen to understanding lighting and positioning your subject we got you covered step by step. One of the most important takeaways from this module will be the role of lighting. Proper lighting can significantly reduce your editing time and if done right your audience won't even know the difference. Lighting is also one of the main factors that can make or break your effect. Applying these basic principles will keep us in the right direction. That being said don't be afraid to jump in and start experimenting. We learn from practice not from watching. Your experience with green screens does not matter, starting with even the simplest setup can lead to incredible results and just remember every great creator started somewhere. Throughout this module we encourage you to test out projects explore your creativity and see for yourself how green screen technology can elevate your work. We're here to help you every step of the way. Are you ready to unlock the full potential of your content? Let's dive in and make some magic happen together. Remember to take actions first then ask questions later. What is a green screen? A green screen is a backdrop used in visual effects to make it simpler to isolate the foreground and replace the background with any digital image. The technique known as chromaking. Lets you create scenes that would otherwise be impossible or too expensive to shoot in real life. The science behind color separation. The selection of green for green screens is grounded in the science of color separation. Digital cameras capture images using red green and blue channels. Green is used most frequently because digital sensors are most sensitive to green wavelengths and it provides a high level of luminance compared to other colors. This makes the green color stand out more distinctly allowing for easier and cleaner separation from the subject. The luminance factor is crucial for defining the edges around the subjects during the keen process reducing the amount of spill and making the compositing process more straightforward. Alternating colors. While green screens are widely used blue screens are also a viable option and were in fact the original choice for film compositing before digital technology. The choice between green and blue screens depends on several factors. Costume and subject color. If the subject or objects in the scene are green or contain green such as plants a blue screen may be used to avoid king out these elements. Lighting conditions. Blue screens can be more beneficial and low light conditions as they require less light to illuminate effectively. Digital sensors and film. Depending on the type of camera and the specific requirement of the shoot blue screens may offer advantages in terms of color reproduction and sensor sensitivity. Choosing the right green screen. Green screens come in various materials including cloth, paper and paint. Cloth screens are portable and can be easily hung or stretched to remove wrinkles. Paper rolls are good disposable option for a clean seamless look but can tear easily. Paint can transform any wall or flat surface into a permanent green screen ideal for dedicated studio spaces. Size and Space Requirements Size of your green screen should be based on the framing of your shots and the movement of your subject. Make sure it's wide and tall enough to cover the entire background and your cameras feel the view with extra space for subjects to move around. Key considerations before purchasing. Opt for a wrinkle resistant material to avoid shadows and uneven lighting. Bright chroma key is typically the best choice for phones and digital cameras. Assess your space and needs, check portable versus permanent setup. Lighting Your Green Screen Proper lighting is crucial in green screen work to ensure a clean key minimizing post production work and achieve a seamless blend between the subject and the background. I'm gonna talk about some techniques that apply across the board and then we'll compare indoor to outdoor setups. Even Illumination The green screen must be lit evenly to avoid shadows and hotspots. This facilitates a smoother king process and post production. Use soft diffuse light sources placed symmetrically on both sides of the screen to achieve uniform lighting. Separate Subject Lighting Let your subject independently from the green screen to control shadows and avoid green spill. The separation ensures the subject is properly exposed and the green screen's color does not reflect onto the subject. Avoiding Shadows and Spill Position the subject at least 6 feet from the green screen to minimize shadows and green spill. Use back lighting or rim light to create a subtle separation between the subject and the background. Using Flags and Reflectors Utilize flags to block unwanted light from hitting the screen and reflectors to fill the shadows of the subject without affecting the green screens lighting. Types of Light and Placement Soft diffused lighting works best to avoid hotspots and shadows on the green screen. LED panels or soft boxes placed on either side of the screen can provide even illumination. The goal is to light the screen separately from the subject to maintain consistent lighting across the entire background. Avoiding Shadows and Hot Spots Position lights at a 45 degree angle to the screen ensuring their evenly spaced and aimed to cover the screen uniformly. Use additional lighting to eliminate shadows cast by the subject. Be mindful of the distance and intensity of the lights to avoid hotspots which making difficult. Ensuring Even Elimination Adjust the lights is needed to eliminate any dark or overly bright areas the more evenly lit the screen the easier it will be to key it out in post production. Indoor Lighting Indoor setups offer controlled conditions making it easier to achieve consistent lighting. Light Sources Soft boxes and LED panels are ideal for indoor use they provide soft even light they can be easily controlled and directed. Screen Lighting Place two light sources at a 45 degree angle to the green screen. Ensuring their equal power and distance from the screen to avoid gradients and shadows. Subject Lighting Use a 3 point lighting setup to illuminate the subject this setup highlights the subjects form and separates them from the background. Outdoor Lighting Outdoor green screen shoots can be challenging due to the variability of natural light and environmental conditions. Timing Overcast days are ideal for outdoor green screen work because the cloud cover offers diffuse natural lighting that evenly lights the screen. Using Natural Light as Fill Use the sun as a backlight or a side light and use reflectors or diffusers to fill in the shadows. This approach can help maintain even lighting on the screen. Wind Instability Secure your green screen and lighting equipment properly to handle wind and other outdoor elements use sandbags and clamps to stabilize your setup. Environmental Considerations Be mindful of changing light conditions and environmental reflections that affect your lighting setup. Regularly monitor and adjust your setup as needed throughout the shoot. Let's talk about positioning your subject. The Distance from the Screen Keeping your subject at least 6 feet from the green screen minimizes green spill which can make king a background more challenging. This distance also helps reduce shadows cast on the screen. Minimizing Green Spill Aside from maintaining distance lighting the subject separately from your green screen can further minimize spill. Use flags or gobos to block the green light from hitting your subject directly. Properly positioning and diffusing your subjects lights can also prevent spill. Conclusion Whether you're working indoors with controlled lighting or outdoors with the challenges of natural light the key to successful green screen lighting lies in achieving even elimination. Separating subject lighting from the screen and minimizing shadows and spill. By mastering these techniques you'll ensure a high quality key and a more professional final product regardless of your filming environment. Let's talk about shooting green screen and camera settings. Exposure Adjust the camera settings to avoid over exposing or under exposing the green screen the goal is to capture a vibrant uniform green that can easily be heat up. Focus Be mindful depth of field. Subject should be in sharp focus while ensuring the green screen remains a consistent color throughout the scene. Resolution In general you wanna have sharper footage to work with when pulling a key. Frame Rate and Shutter Speed Motion blur can be problematic and cause less than appealing results. Higher frame rates and shutter speed will help eliminate motion blur. If your subject is moving around a lot you're gonna wanna opt for higher frame rates and shutter speeds 30 frames per second or higher. If shooting with your phone you may want to use a third party app so you have full control over your settings. Background and Foreground Integration Lens Selection Depending on the size of your green screen you'll need to consider the focal length of your lens if you have a small screen you typically need to place your camera farther back from the subject with a larger focal length to keep the subject visually within the screen. If using your cell phone you might be able to just pull your tripod back and change it to 2 times zoom if you have a larger screen you'll have more flexibility and creative control. Matching Elements When planning your shots consider how the subjects will interact with the intended background and post production. This includes matching the direction of the lighting, the color temperature and perspective of the camera. If you can use the same lighting setup for both scenes for a seamless integration.
